network.overall_strategy: This network centers the literature-supported glycolaldehyde-to-serinonitrile Strecker manifold as the dominant route to serine, while using hydrothermal chemistry mainly to supply reduced carbon feedstocks and surface settings to generate and concentrate glycolaldehyde, HCN, and protected aminonitrile intermediates. Ten distinct pathways are encoded as different valid traversals through a connected bipartite graph. Convergence occurs primarily at formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, serinonitrile, and serine. The network also captures literature-supported branch chemistry linking serine to dehydroalanine nitrile/cysteine chemistry, phosphoserine-related systems chemistry, peptide capture, and known decomposition back to pyruvate or glycine plus formaldehyde. Hot hydrothermal settings are not used as major direct serine-forming environments because the literature indicates serine is thermally fragile there.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate on iron-sulfur minerals

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Surface-mediated reduction of CO2 by H2 on mixed-valence iron-sulfur mineral surfaces gives formate as an early fixed-carbon product.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite and related Fe-S phases activate CO2 and support its reduction under hydrothermal conditions; this is consistent with iron-sulfur world carbon fixation and DFT/mechanistic studies of greigite catalysis (Santos-Carballal et al., 2018; Wächtershäuser, 1988).

reactions.name: Surface photoreduction of CO2/formate pool to formaldehyde equivalents

conditions.other: UV-irradiated shallow pool, anoxic, concentrated by evaporation

reactions.mechanism: Photocatalytic reduction of oxidized one-carbon species yields formaldehyde-equivalent reduced carbon in surface waters.
reactions.reasoning: Direct formaldehyde generation is treated here as a necessary feeder abstraction from reduced one-carbon photochemistry rather than a single sharply defined laboratory step. UV-driven mineral photoreduction of CO2 to reduced C1 species is supported generally for semiconductor minerals, while concentration on clays improves retention; this connection is speculative but constrained to feed literature-supported downstream formose/Strecker chemistry (Guzman and Martin, 2010; Mulkidjanian, 2009; Breslow, 1959).

reactions.name: Surface generation of HCN and formaldimine precursor pool

conditions.other: UV irradiation, anoxic, atmospheric/surface interface

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical and redox processing of reduced C1/N pools furnishes HCN and methanimine/formaldimine precursors that enter Strecker-type chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The literature emphasizes that spark discharge and related atmospheric photochemistry generate both HCN and aldehydes, and recent computation suggests formaldimine can be a kinetically favorable precursor in serine-forming manifolds. This reaction therefore encodes the empirically supported HCN supply and the literature-noted formaldimine possibility as a connected surface precursor pool (Miller, 1957; Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Shoji et al., 2023; JACS Au, 2024 computational study referenced in the report).

reactions.name: Formose initiation to glycolaldehyde from formaldehyde

conditions.other: basic evaporitic pool; wet-dry concentration; borate stabilization of sugar products

reactions.mechanism: Autocatalytic aldol/formose chemistry converts formaldehyde into the first stable C2 sugar, glycolaldehyde, which is stabilized by borate-rich mineral environments.
reactions.reasoning: Glycolaldehyde is the critical aldehyde bottleneck for serine Strecker synthesis, and its prebiotic generation via the Butlerov formose reaction is well established; borate minerals improve sugar stability in surface settings (Breslow, 1959; the report citing 2023 olivine-catalyzed glycolaldehyde formation; Jørgensen et al., 2012 for extraterrestrial availability).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox homologation to glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Cu photoredox cycle in phosphate-buffered sulfide-rich setting represented here with sulfide/phosphate mineral environment
conditions.other: 35C, neutral phosphate buffer, UV irradiation, anaerobic

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven cyanosulfidic reductive homologation of HCN generates C2 and C3 sugars, especially glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde, under mild surface conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. showed that HCN and H2S under UV photoredox conditions produce glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde and that aminonitrile precursors to serine are inevitable by-products of this network; phosphate-buffered surface settings are explicit in the study (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Canonical Strecker formation of serinonitrile from glycolaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration on clay and borate-rich surfaces
conditions.other: mildly basic aqueous conditions; equilibrium imine formation followed by cyanide addition

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine, followed by nucleophilic addition of HCN to form 2-amino-3-hydroxypropanenitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the dominant literature-supported route to prebiotic serine. Glycolaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN form serinonitrile through the classic Strecker sequence, with aminonitrile formation as the first thermodynamically stable intermediate (Miller and Van Trump, 1981; Magrino, Pietrucci, and Saitta, 2021).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of serinonitrile to serinamide

conditions.other: cold to mild aqueous pool; geologically rapid over <1000 years at 0C

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydration and partial hydrolysis yields the corresponding amide as the first irreversible hydrolysis product.
reactions.reasoning: Aminonitrile hydrolysis kinetics were measured for prebiotic systems and proceed on geologically short timescales; serinamide is the accepted intermediate on the route from serinonitrile to serine (Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of serinamide to serine

conditions.other: cool aqueous conditions favored for survival of serine

reactions.mechanism: Further amide hydrolysis gives the free amino acid serine.
reactions.reasoning: The second hydrolysis step from serinamide to serine is the established completion of the Strecker manifold and is favored in cool aqueous surface environments relative to hot hydrothermal ones where serine is unstable (Miller and Van Trump, 1981; Qian et al., 1993; Bada, Miller, and Zhao, 1995).

reactions.name: Formamide-protected serinonitrile formation

conditions.catalyst: MgCl2-promoted formamide chemistry in mineral-rich drying environment
conditions.other: formamide solvent; around 80C; drying pool chemistry

reactions.mechanism: In formamide-rich media, glycolaldehyde and cyanide generate N-formyl-protected serinonitrile derivatives, reducing reversion of the aminonitrile equilibrium.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. showed that N-formylserinonitrile forms readily in formamide and that formyl protection stabilizes the aminonitrile manifold; MgCl2 promoted related dehydroalanine nitrile chemistry at 80C. This is a direct literature-supported branch of serine prebiotic chemistry (Pulletikurti et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Hydrolytic deprotection and conversion of N-formylserinonitrile to serine

conditions.other: re-entry into aqueous surface pool after drying/formamide phase

reactions.mechanism: Protected aminonitrile undergoes hydrolytic deprotection and nitrile hydrolysis to furnish serine under milder aqueous conditions.
reactions.reasoning: The literature specifically highlights N-formylserinonitrile as a protective intermediate that suppresses back-reaction and can feed productive hydrolysis toward serine. This reaction encodes that experimentally motivated conversion in a connected network form (Pulletikurti et al., 2023).

reactions.name: β-Elimination of serinonitrile to dehydroalanine nitrile

conditions.catalyst: mineral-assisted elimination in drying or formamide-rich environment
conditions.other: compatible with nitrile-activated dehydration chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Serinonitrile loses water-equivalent functionality through nitrile-activated elimination to form dehydroalanine nitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Foden et al. demonstrated the serine-to-cysteine manifold through nitrile-activated dehydroalanine synthesis, identifying dehydroalanine nitrile as a central branch-point intermediate downstream of serinonitrile chemistry (Foden et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Sulfide addition and hydrolysis from dehydroalanine nitrile to cysteine

conditions.other: neutral aqueous solution; sulfide-rich

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of sulfide to dehydroalanine nitrile followed by hydrolysis yields cysteine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the experimentally established prebiotic serine-to-cysteine connection via dehydroalanine nitrile, notable because it parallels biology while relying on nitrile activation rather than enzymes (Foden et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Phosphate-controlled network to phosphoserine and pyruvate

conditions.other: alpha-phosphorylation-controlled aqueous network; mild surface conditions

reactions.mechanism: Interlinked phosphorylation and carbonyl chemistry of glycolaldehyde/glyceraldehyde gives a systems-level product set including phosphoserine-related species and pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is intentionally encoded as a network node rather than a single elementary step because the cited literature reports a systems chemistry manifold producing phosphoserine, glyceric acid phosphates, and pyruvate together. It is included exactly to reflect that experimentally described network behavior without inventing unsupported direct serine formation (Powner group Nature Chemistry, 2017; Powner et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Dephosphorylation of phosphoserine pool to serine

conditions.other: hydrolytic release in prebiotic pool or mineral interface

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic cleavage of phosphate from phosphoserine-like species releases free serine.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports phosphoserine as part of a broader prebiotic phosphorylation network connected to glycolytic and nucleotide chemistry. Conversion to free serine is included as a necessary downstream hydrolytic step to connect the experimentally observed phosphoserine pool to the target; this step is plausible but less directly demonstrated than the canonical Strecker route and should be considered network-level rather than elementary (Powner group Nature Chemistry, 2017).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from reduced carbon

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent gradients; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-catalyzed reductive carbon fixation and C-C coupling generate alpha-keto acid products including pyruvate under vent-like conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal mineral systems plausibly generate pyruvate-like central carbon intermediates, and pyruvate is explicitly identified in connected phosphate-controlled prebiotic networks. This reaction is included as a supported hydrothermal feed into the broader serine network rather than a direct serine route (Kitadai, 2015; Barge et al., 2019; report summary on pyruvate synthesis in vent settings).

reactions.name: Thermal decomposition of serine to pyruvate

conditions.other: heating during geothermal cycling; dehydration pathway dominant

reactions.mechanism: Serine dehydrates to aminoacrylate-like intermediates that ultimately yield pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Serine decomposition to pyruvate is the dominant thermal degradation pathway and an important constraint on plausible prebiotic settings, so it is represented explicitly as a connected branch in the network (Körner et al., 2021; Al-Kwradi et al., 2024; Andersson and Holm, 2000).

reactions.name: Retro-aldol decomposition of serine to glycine and formaldehyde

conditions.other: thermal stress during wet-dry or geothermal cycling

reactions.mechanism: Retro-aldol cleavage of the beta-hydroxyl amino acid yields glycine and formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This decomposition route is explicitly discussed in the literature and helps explain why glycine can appear upon serine heating. Including it also creates a realistic recycling loop back to formaldehyde/formose chemistry (Körner et al., 2021; Al-Kwradi et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Montmorillonite-assisted peptide capture of serine with glycine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling on clay-rich mineral interfaces; H2S-mediated peptide ligation relevance

reactions.mechanism: Dehydrative condensation or aminonitrile-enabled ligation captures serine into short peptides, protecting it from degradation and moving chemistry toward proto-biopolymers.
reactions.reasoning: Clay-assisted peptide formation and aminonitrile-mediated ligation are both experimentally supported prebiotic routes. Serine-containing residues participate in H2S-mediated peptide ligation, making peptide capture a realistic downstream fate (Bujdák and Rode, 1999; Huber and Wächtershäuser, 1998; Canavelli, Islam, and Powner, 2019).

reactions.name: Aminothiazole sequestration and release of glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: crystallization-purification cycle in evaporitic pools

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde is transiently sequestered as 2-aminothiazole aminals and later released, increasing persistence and purification of the key aldehyde feedstock.
reactions.reasoning: Powner and coworkers identified 2-aminothiazole aminals as a plausible accumulation and purification mechanism for glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde. The node is written as a reservoir/release transformation rather than net production to avoid overstating chemistry while preserving the experimentally described concentration mechanism (Powner et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge atmospheric source feeding the Strecker manifold

conditions.other: electric discharge in reduced volcanic atmosphere followed by dissolution into aqueous phase

reactions.mechanism: Atmospheric discharge generates aldehydes and HCN that dissolve into water and undergo Strecker conversion to serinonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Spark-discharge experiments detected serine and support a Strecker-based mechanism in which HCN and aldehydes accumulate first and amino acids rise later. This node uses the experimentally grounded downstream condensation outcome rather than an unrealistic single-step direct serine synthesis (Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011; Miller, 1957).

pathways.name: Canonical surface Strecker route
pathways.description: Formaldehyde forms glycolaldehyde by formose chemistry, which undergoes Strecker synthesis to serinonitrile and sequential hydrolysis to serine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface Strecker route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction supplies formate, which is photochemically reduced to formaldehyde in surface pools before entering the canonical glycolaldehyde Strecker sequence.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic inevitable serine route
pathways.description: HCN and H2S under UV photoredox conditions generate glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde, feeding serinonitrile formation and hydrolysis to serine.

pathways.name: Protected formamide serine route
pathways.description: Glycolaldehyde enters formamide-rich chemistry to form N-formylserinonitrile, which is hydrolyzed to serine after protection-assisted persistence.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-feed plus protected formamide route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced carbon supplies formaldehyde equivalents that become glycolaldehyde and then N-formylserinonitrile in drying formamide environments before conversion to serine.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge-fed Strecker route
pathways.description: Electric-discharge chemistry generates the aldehyde/HCN pool, represented here by direct entry into serinonitrile formation, followed by hydrolysis to serine.

pathways.name: HCN/formaldimine-assisted entry to glycolaldehyde Strecker chemistry
pathways.description: Surface photochemistry generates HCN and formaldimine alongside formaldehyde, supporting glycolaldehyde generation and then the canonical serine Strecker route.

pathways.name: Aminothiazole-reservoir-controlled serine route
pathways.description: Glycolaldehyde is accumulated and purified through aminothiazole sequestration cycles before release into the Strecker manifold leading to serine.

pathways.name: Phosphate-controlled phosphoserine route
pathways.description: Cyanosulfidic sugars feed a phosphorylation-controlled systems chemistry network that yields a phosphoserine pool, which hydrolyzes to serine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-carbon-supported phosphate route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation supplies reduced feedstocks that connect to surface sugar chemistry and then into the phosphate-controlled phosphoserine network yielding serine.
